([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἑλλάς, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *Hellás*) was a northeastern [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") civilization, existing from the [Greek Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages") of the 12th–9th centuries BC to the end of [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") (c. 600 AD), that comprised a loose collection of culturally and linguistically related [city-states](/wiki/Polis "Polis") and communities. Prior to the [Roman period](/wiki/Greece_in_the_Roman_era "Greece in the Roman era"), most of these regions were officially unified only once under the [Kingdom of Macedon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Macedon "Kingdom of Macedon") from 338 to 323 BC.[[a]](#cite_note-1) In [Western history](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), the era of classical antiquity was immediately followed by the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") and the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") period.[[1]](#cite_note-Thomas1988-2)

**Ancient Greece** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἑλλάς, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *Hellás*) was a northeastern [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") civilization, existing from the [Greek Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages") of the 12th–9th centuries BC to the end of [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") (c. 600 AD), that comprised a loose collection of culturally and linguistically related [city-states](/wiki/Polis "Polis") and communities. Prior to the [Roman period](/wiki/Greece_in_the_Roman_era "Greece in the Roman era"), most of these regions were officially unified only once under the [Kingdom of Macedon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Macedon "Kingdom of Macedon") from 338 to 323 BC.[[a]](#cite_note-1) In [Western history](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), the era of classical antiquity was immediately followed by the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") and the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") period.[[1]](#cite_note-Thomas1988-2)

Three centuries after the decline of [Mycenaean Greece](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greece "Mycenaean Greece") during the [Bronze Age collapse](/wiki/Bronze_Age_collapse "Bronze Age collapse"), Greek urban [poleis](/wiki/Poleis "Poleis") began to form in the 8th century BC, ushering in the [Archaic period](/wiki/Archaic_Greece "Archaic Greece") and [the colonization](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation") of the Mediterranean Basin. This was followed by the age of [Classical Greece](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece"), from the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars") to the death of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") in 323 BC, and which included the [Golden Age of Athens](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Athens "Golden Age of Athens") and the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") between [Athens](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens") and [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta"). The unification of Greece by [Macedon](/wiki/Rise_of_Macedon "Rise of Macedon") under [Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Macedon "Philip II of Macedon") and subsequent conquest of the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") by Alexander the Great spread Hellenistic civilization across the Middle East. The [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") is considered to have ended in 30 BC, when the last Hellenistic kingdom, [Ptolemaic Egypt](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Egypt "Ptolemaic Egypt"), was annexed by the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic").

Classical [Greek culture](/wiki/Greek_culture "Greek culture"), especially philosophy, had a powerful influence on [ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), which carried a version of it throughout the Mediterranean and much of Europe. For this reason, Classical Greece is generally considered the [cradle](/wiki/Cradle_of_civilization "Cradle of civilization") of [Western civilization](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), the [seminal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/seminal "wikt:seminal") culture from which the modern West derives many of its founding archetypes and ideas in politics, philosophy, science, and art.[[2]](#cite_note-EllynMcGinnis2004-3)[[3]](#cite_note-FindlingPelle2004-4)[[4]](#cite_note-ThompsonMullin-5)

## Chronology

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of ancient Greece](/wiki/Timeline_of_ancient_Greece "Timeline of ancient Greece").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Parthenon_%2830276156187%29.jpg/250px-Parthenon_%2830276156187%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parthenon_(30276156187).jpg)

The [Parthenon](/wiki/Parthenon "Parthenon"), a temple dedicated to [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena"), located on the [Acropolis](/wiki/Acropolis "Acropolis") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), is one of the most representative symbols of the culture and sophistication of the ancient Greeks.

[Classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") in the Mediterranean region is commonly considered to have begun in the 8th century BC[[5]](#cite_note-6) (around the time of the earliest recorded poetry of Homer) and ended in the 6th century AD.

Classical antiquity in Greece was preceded by the [Greek Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages") (c. 1200 – c. 800 BC), [archaeologically](/wiki/Archaeologically "Archaeologically") characterised by the [protogeometric](/wiki/Protogeometric "Protogeometric") and [geometric styles](/wiki/Geometric_art "Geometric art") of designs on pottery. Following the Dark Ages was the [Archaic period](/wiki/Archaic_Greece "Archaic Greece"), beginning around the 8th century BC, which saw early developments in Greek culture and society leading to the [Classical period](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece")[[6]](#cite_note-7) from the Persian invasion of Greece in 480 BC until the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC.[[7]](#cite_note-8) The Classical period is characterized by a "classical" style, i.e. one which was considered exemplary by later observers, most famously in the [Parthenon](/wiki/Parthenon "Parthenon") of Athens. Politically, the Classical period was dominated by [Athens](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens") and the [Delian League](/wiki/Delian_League "Delian League") during the 5th century, but displaced by [Spartan hegemony](/wiki/Spartan_hegemony "Spartan hegemony") during the early 4th century BC, before power shifted to [Thebes](/wiki/Ancient_Thebes_(Boeotia) "Ancient Thebes (Boeotia)") and the [Boeotian League](/wiki/Boeotian_League "Boeotian League") and finally to the [League of Corinth](/wiki/League_of_Corinth "League of Corinth") led by [Macedon](/wiki/Macedon "Macedon"). This period was shaped by the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars"), the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War"), and the [Rise of Macedon](/wiki/Rise_of_Macedon "Rise of Macedon").

Following the Classical period was the Hellenistic period (323–146 BC), during which Greek culture and power expanded into the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") from the death of Alexander until the Roman conquest. [Roman Greece](/wiki/Roman_Greece "Roman Greece") is usually counted from the Roman victory over the [Corinthians](/wiki/Corinth "Corinth") at the [Battle of Corinth](/wiki/Battle_of_Corinth_(146_BC) "Battle of Corinth (146 BC)") in 146 BC to the establishment of [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") by [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") as the capital of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in 330 AD. Finally, [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") refers to the period of [Christianization](/wiki/Decline_of_Greco-Roman_polytheism "Decline of Greco-Roman polytheism") during the later 4th to early 6th centuries AD, consummated by the closure of the [Academy of Athens](/wiki/Plato%27s_Academy "Plato's Academy") by [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") in 529.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## Historiography

Main article: [Greek historiographers](/wiki/Greek_historiographers "Greek historiographers")

The historical period of ancient Greece is unique in world history as the first period attested directly in comprehensive, narrative [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography"), while earlier ancient history or [protohistory](/wiki/Protohistory "Protohistory") is known from much more fragmentary documents such as annals, king lists, and pragmatic [epigraphy](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy").

[Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") is widely known as the "father of history": his *[Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)")* are eponymous of [the entire field](/wiki/History "History"). Written between the 450s and 420s BC, Herodotus' work reaches about a century into the past, discussing 6th-century BC historical figures such as [Darius I of Persia](/wiki/Darius_I_of_Persia "Darius I of Persia"), [Cambyses II](/wiki/Cambyses_II "Cambyses II") and [Psamtik III](/wiki/Psamtik_III "Psamtik III"), and alluding to some 8th-century BC persons such as [Candaules](/wiki/Candaules "Candaules"). The accuracy of Herodotus' works is debated.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Herodotus was succeeded by authors such as [Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides"), [Xenophon](/wiki/Xenophon "Xenophon"), [Demosthenes](/wiki/Demosthenes "Demosthenes"), [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") and [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"). Most were either Athenian or pro-Athenian, which is why far more is known about the history and politics of Athens than of many other cities.
Their scope is further limited by a focus on political, military and diplomatic history, ignoring economic and social history.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## History

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of ancient Greece](/wiki/Timeline_of_ancient_Greece "Timeline of ancient Greece").

Further information: [History of Greece](/wiki/History_of_Greece "History of Greece")

### Archaic period

Main article: [Archaic Greece](/wiki/Archaic_Greece "Archaic Greece")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Prothesis_Dipylon_Painter_Louvre_A517.jpg/330px-Prothesis_Dipylon_Painter_Louvre_A517.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prothesis_Dipylon_Painter_Louvre_A517.jpg)

Dipylon Vase of the late [Geometric period](/wiki/Geometric_art "Geometric art"), or the beginning of the Archaic period, c. 750 BC.

The archaic period, lasting approximately from 800 to 500 BC, saw the culmination of political and social developments which had begun in the Greek Dark Age, with the *polis* (city-state) becoming the most important unit of political organisation in Greece.[[15]](#cite_note-16) The absence of powerful states in Greece after the collapse of Mycenaean power, and the geography of Greece, where many settlements were separated from their neighbours by mountainous terrain, encouraged the development of small independent city-states.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Several Greek states saw tyrants rise to power in this period, most famously at [Corinth](/wiki/Ancient_Corinth "Ancient Corinth") from 657 BC.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The period also saw the founding of Greek colonies around the Mediterranean, with [Euboean](/wiki/Euboea "Euboea") settlements at [Al-Mina](/wiki/Al-Mina "Al-Mina") in the east as early as 800 BC, and [Ischia](/wiki/Ischia "Ischia") in the west by 775.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Increasing contact with non-Greek peoples in this period, especially in the Near East, inspired developments in art and architecture, the adoption of coinage, and the development of the Greek alphabet.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Athens developed its democratic system over the course of the archaic period. Already in the 7th century, the right of all citizen men to attend the [assembly](/wiki/Ecclesia_(ancient_Greece) "Ecclesia (ancient Greece)") appears to have been established.[[20]](#cite_note-21) After a failed coup led by [Cylon of Athens](/wiki/Cylon_of_Athens "Cylon of Athens") around 636 BC, [Draco](/wiki/Draco_(lawgiver) "Draco (lawgiver)") was appointed to establish a code of laws in 621. This failed to reduce the political tension between the poor and the elites, and in 594 [Solon](/wiki/Solon "Solon") was given the authority to enact another set of reforms, which attempted to balance the power of the rich and the poor.[[21]](#cite_note-22) In the middle of the 6th century, [Pisistratus](/wiki/Pisistratus "Pisistratus") established himself as a tyrant, and after his death in 527 his son [Hippias](/wiki/Hippias "Hippias") inherited his position; by the end of the 6th century he had been overthrown, and [Cleisthenes](/wiki/Cleisthenes "Cleisthenes") carried out further democratising reforms.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

In Sparta, a political system with two kings, a [council of elders](/wiki/Gerousia "Gerousia"), and five [ephors](/wiki/Ephors "Ephors") developed over the course of the 8th and 7th centuries. According to Spartan tradition, this constitution was established by the legendary lawgiver [Lycurgus](/wiki/Lycurgus "Lycurgus").[[23]](#cite_note-24) Over the course of the [First Messenian War](/wiki/First_Messenian_War "First Messenian War") and [Second Messenian War](/wiki/Second_Messenian_War "Second Messenian War"), Sparta subjugated the neighbouring region of [Messenia](/wiki/Messenia "Messenia"), enserfing the population.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In the 6th century, Greek city-states began to develop formal relationships with one another, where previously individual rulers had relied on personal relationships with the elites of other cities.[[25]](#cite_note-26) Towards the end of the Archaic period, Sparta began to build a series of alliances, the [Peloponnesian League](/wiki/Peloponnesian_League "Peloponnesian League"), with cities including [Corinth](/wiki/Corinth "Corinth"), [Elis](/wiki/Elis "Elis"), and [Megara](/wiki/Megara "Megara"),[[26]](#cite_note-27) isolating Messenia and reinforcing Sparta's position against [Argos](/wiki/Argos,_Peloponnese "Argos, Peloponnese"), the other major power in the Peloponnese.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Other alliances in the 6th century included those between Elis and [Heraea](/wiki/Heraea_(Arcadia) "Heraea (Arcadia)") in the Peloponnese; and between the Greek colony [Sybaris](/wiki/Sybaris "Sybaris") in southern Italy, its allies, and the Serdaioi.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Classical Greece

Main article: [Classical Greece](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/EarlyAthenianCoin.jpg/250px-EarlyAthenianCoin.jpg)](/wiki/File:EarlyAthenianCoin.jpg)

Early Athenian coin, depicting the head of [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena") on the obverse and her owl on the reverse – 5th century BC

In 499 BC, the [Ionian](/wiki/Ionia "Ionia") city-states under Persian rule rebelled against their Persian-supported tyrant rulers.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Supported by troops sent from Athens and [Eretria](/wiki/Eretria "Eretria"), they advanced as far as [Sardis](/wiki/Sardis "Sardis") and burnt the city before being driven back by a Persian counterattack.[[30]](#cite_note-martin2013-31) The revolt continued until 494, when the rebelling Ionians were defeated.[[30]](#cite_note-martin2013-31) Darius did not forget that Athens had assisted the Ionian revolt, and in 490 he assembled an armada to retaliate.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Though heavily outnumbered, the Athenians—supported by their [Plataean](/wiki/Plataea "Plataea") allies—defeated the Persian hordes at the [Battle of Marathon](/wiki/Battle_of_Marathon "Battle of Marathon"), and the Persian fleet turned tail.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Map_Greco-Persian_Wars-en.svg/500px-Map_Greco-Persian_Wars-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Greco-Persian_Wars-en.svg)

Events of the first phases of the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Map_athenian_empire_431_BC-en.svg/500px-Map_athenian_empire_431_BC-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_athenian_empire_431_BC-en.svg)

The Delian League immediately before the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") in 431 BC

Ten years later, a [second invasion](/wiki/Second_Persian_invasion_of_Greece "Second Persian invasion of Greece") was launched by Darius' son [Xerxes](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I").[[33]](#cite_note-martin131-34) The city-states of northern and central Greece submitted to the Persian forces without resistance, but a coalition of 31 Greek city-states, including Athens and Sparta, determined to resist the Persian invaders.[[33]](#cite_note-martin131-34) At the same time, Greek Sicily was invaded by a Carthaginian force.[[33]](#cite_note-martin131-34) In 480 BC, the first major battle of the invasion was fought at [Thermopylae](/wiki/Battle_of_Thermopylae "Battle of Thermopylae"), where a small rearguard of Greeks, led by three hundred Spartans, held a crucial pass guarding the heart of Greece for several days; at the same time [Gelon](/wiki/Gelo "Gelo"), tyrant of Syracuse, defeated the Carthaginian invasion at the [Battle of Himera](/wiki/Battle_of_Himera_(480_BC) "Battle of Himera (480 BC)").[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The Persians were decisively defeated at sea by a primarily Athenian naval force at the [Battle of Salamis](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamis "Battle of Salamis"), and on land in 479 BC at the [Battle of Plataea](/wiki/Battle_of_Plataea "Battle of Plataea").[[35]](#cite_note-36) The alliance against Persia continued, initially led by the Spartan [Pausanias](/wiki/Pausanias_(general) "Pausanias (general)") but from 477 by Athens,[[36]](#cite_note-37) and by 460 Persia had been driven out of the Aegean.[[37]](#cite_note-38) During this long campaign, the [Delian League](/wiki/Delian_League "Delian League") gradually transformed from a defensive alliance of Greek states into an Athenian empire, as Athens' growing naval power intimidated the other league states.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Athens ended its campaigns against Persia in 450, after a disastrous defeat in Egypt in 454, and the death of [Cimon](/wiki/Cimon "Cimon") in action against the Persians on Cyprus in 450.[[39]](#cite_note-martin147-40)

As the Athenian fight against the Persian empire waned, conflict grew between Athens and Sparta. Suspicious of the increasing Athenian power funded by the Delian League, Sparta offered aid to reluctant members of the League to rebel against Athenian domination. These tensions were exacerbated in 462 BC when Athens sent a force to aid Sparta in overcoming a [helot](/wiki/Helot "Helot") revolt, but this aid was rejected by the Spartans.[[40]](#cite_note-41) In the 450s, Athens took control of Boeotia, and won victories over [Aegina](/wiki/Aegina "Aegina") and Corinth.[[39]](#cite_note-martin147-40) However, Athens failed to win a decisive victory, and in 447 lost Boeotia again.[[39]](#cite_note-martin147-40) Athens and Sparta signed the [Thirty Years' Peace](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_Peace "Thirty Years' Peace") in the winter of 446/445, ending the conflict.[[39]](#cite_note-martin147-40)

Despite the treaty, Athenian relations with Sparta declined again in the 430s, and in 431 BC the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") began.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The [first phase of the war](/wiki/Archidamian_War "Archidamian War") saw a series of fruitless annual invasions of Attica by Sparta, while Athens successfully fought the Corinthian empire in northwest Greece and defended its own empire, despite a [plague](/wiki/Plague_of_Athens "Plague of Athens") which killed the leading Athenian statesman [Pericles](/wiki/Pericles "Pericles").[[42]](#cite_note-hornblower160-43) The war turned after Athenian victories led by [Cleon](/wiki/Cleon "Cleon") at [Pylos](/wiki/Battle_of_Pylos "Battle of Pylos") and [Sphakteria](/wiki/Battle_of_Sphacteria "Battle of Sphacteria"),[[42]](#cite_note-hornblower160-43) and Sparta sued for peace, but the Athenians rejected the proposal.[[43]](#cite_note-hornblower162-44) The Athenian failure to regain control of Boeotia at [Delium](/wiki/Battle_of_Delium "Battle of Delium") and [Brasidas](/wiki/Brasidas "Brasidas")' successes in northern Greece in 424 improved Sparta's position after Sphakteria.[[43]](#cite_note-hornblower162-44) After the deaths of Cleon and Brasidas, the strongest proponents of war on each side, [a peace treaty](/wiki/Peace_of_Nikias "Peace of Nikias") was negotiated in 421 by the Athenian general [Nicias](/wiki/Nicias "Nicias").[[44]](#cite_note-45)

The peace did not last, however. In 418 BC allied forces of Athens and Argos were defeated by Sparta at [Mantinea](/wiki/Battle_of_Mantinea_(418_BC) "Battle of Mantinea (418 BC)").[[45]](#cite_note-46) In 415 Athens launched an ambitious [naval expedition](/wiki/Sicilian_Expedition "Sicilian Expedition") to dominate Sicily;[[46]](#cite_note-47) the expedition ended in disaster at the harbor of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily"), with almost the entire army killed, and the ships destroyed.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Soon after the Athenian defeat in Syracuse, Athens' Ionian allies began to rebel against the Delian league, while Persia began to once again involve itself in Greek affairs on the Spartan side.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Initially the Athenian position continued relatively strong, with important victories at [Cyzicus](/wiki/Battle_of_Cyzicus "Battle of Cyzicus") in 410 and [Arginusae](/wiki/Battle_of_Arginusae "Battle of Arginusae") in 406.[[49]](#cite_note-50) However, in 405 the Spartan [Lysander](/wiki/Lysander "Lysander") defeated Athens in the [Battle of Aegospotami](/wiki/Battle_of_Aegospotami "Battle of Aegospotami"), and began to blockade Athens' harbour;[[50]](#cite_note-Martin_2013_205-51) driven by hunger, Athens sued for peace, agreeing to surrender their fleet and join the Spartan-led Peloponnesian League.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Following the Athenian surrender, Sparta installed an oligarchic regime, the [Thirty Tyrants](/wiki/Thirty_Tyrants "Thirty Tyrants"), in Athens,[[50]](#cite_note-Martin_2013_205-51) one of a number of Spartan-backed oligarchies which rose to power after the Peloponnesian war.[[52]](#cite_note-53) Spartan predominance did not last: after only a year, the Thirty had been overthrown.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The first half of the 4th century saw the major Greek states attempt to dominate the mainland; none were successful, and their resulting weakness led to a power vacuum which was eventually filled by Macedon under Philip II and then Alexander the Great.[[54]](#cite_note-55) In the immediate aftermath of the Peloponnesian war, Sparta attempted to extend their own power, leading Argos, Athens, Corinth, and Thebes to join against them.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Aiming to prevent any single Greek state gaining the dominance that would allow it to challenge Persia, the Persian king initially joined the alliance against Sparta, before imposing the [Peace of Antalcidas](/wiki/Peace_of_Antalcidas "Peace of Antalcidas") ("King's Peace") which restored Persia's control over the Anatolian Greeks.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/L%27atleta_di_Fano.jpg/250px-L%27atleta_di_Fano.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27atleta_di_Fano.jpg)

The *[Victorious Youth](/wiki/Victorious_Youth "Victorious Youth")* (c. 310 BC) is a rare, water-preserved [bronze sculpture](/wiki/Bronze_sculpture "Bronze sculpture") from ancient Greece.

By 371 BC, Thebes was in the ascendancy, defeating Sparta at the [Battle of Leuctra](/wiki/Battle_of_Leuctra "Battle of Leuctra"), killing the Spartan king [Cleombrotus I](/wiki/Cleombrotus_I "Cleombrotus I"), and invading Laconia. Further Theban successes against Sparta in 369 led to Messenia gaining independence; Sparta never recovered from the loss of Messenia's fertile land and the helot workforce it provided.[[57]](#cite_note-58) The rising power of Thebes led Sparta and Athens to join forces; in 362 they were defeated by Thebes at the [Battle of Mantinea](/wiki/Battle_of_Mantinea_(362_BC) "Battle of Mantinea (362 BC)"). In the aftermath of Mantinea, none of the major Greek states were able to dominate. Though Thebes had won the battle, their general Epaminondas was killed, and they spent the following decades embroiled in wars with their neighbours; Athens, meanwhile, saw its second naval alliance, formed in 377, collapse in the mid-350s.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

The power vacuum in Greece after the Battle of Mantinea was filled by Macedon, under [Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Macedon "Philip II of Macedon"), starting from the [battle of Crocus field](/wiki/Battle_of_Crocus_field "Battle of Crocus field"). In 338 BC, he defeated a Greek alliance at the [Battle of Chaeronea](/wiki/Battle_of_Chaeronea_(338_BC) "Battle of Chaeronea (338 BC)"), and subsequently formed the [League of Corinth](/wiki/League_of_Corinth "League of Corinth"). Philip planned to lead the League to invade Persia, but was murdered in 336 BC. His son [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") was left to fulfill his father's ambitions.[[59]](#cite_note-60) After campaigns against Macedon's western and northern enemies, and those Greek states that had broken from the League of Corinth following the death of Philip, Alexander began his campaign against Persia in 334 BC.[[60]](#cite_note-61) He conquered Persia, defeating [Darius III](/wiki/Darius_III "Darius III") at the [Battle of Issus](/wiki/Battle_of_Issus "Battle of Issus") in 333 BC, and after the [Battle of Gaugamela](/wiki/Battle_of_Gaugamela "Battle of Gaugamela") in 331 BC proclaimed himself king of Asia.[[61]](#cite_note-62) From 329 BC he led expeditions to [Bactria](/wiki/Wars_of_Alexander_the_Great#Bactria "Wars of Alexander the Great") and then [India](/wiki/Indian_campaign_of_Alexander_the_Great "Indian campaign of Alexander the Great");[[62]](#cite_note-63) further plans to invade Arabia and North Africa were halted by his death in 323 BC.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### Hellenistic Greece

Main articles: [Wars of Alexander the Great](/wiki/Wars_of_Alexander_the_Great "Wars of Alexander the Great"), [Diadochi](/wiki/Diadochi "Diadochi"), [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period"), and [Hellenistic Greece](/wiki/Hellenistic_Greece "Hellenistic Greece")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Alexander_the_Great_mosaic.jpg/250px-Alexander_the_Great_mosaic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexander_the_Great_mosaic.jpg)

[Alexander Mosaic](/wiki/Alexander_Mosaic "Alexander Mosaic"), National Archaeological Museum, Naples

The period from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC until the death of [Cleopatra](/wiki/Cleopatra "Cleopatra"), the last Macedonian ruler of Egypt, is known as the Hellenistic period. In the early part of this period, a new form of kingship developed based on Macedonian and Near Eastern traditions. The first Hellenistic kings were previously Alexander's generals, and took power in the period following his death, though they were not part of existing royal lineages and lacked historic claims to the territories they controlled.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin2013253-65) The most important of these rulers in the decades after Alexander's death were [Antigonus I](/wiki/Antigonus_I "Antigonus I") and his son [Demetrius](/wiki/Demetrius_I_of_Macedon "Demetrius I of Macedon") in Macedonia and the rest of Greece, [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy_I_Soter "Ptolemy I Soter") in Egypt, and [Seleucus I](/wiki/Seleucus_I "Seleucus I") in Syria and the former Persian empire;[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin2013254–255-66) smaller Hellenistic kingdoms included [Epirus](/wiki/Epirus_(ancient_state) "Epirus (ancient state)") under the reign of [Pyrrhus](/wiki/Pyrrhus_of_Epirus "Pyrrhus of Epirus"), the [Attalids](/wiki/Attalid "Attalid") in Anatolia and the [Greco-Bactrian kingdom](/wiki/Greco-Bactrian_kingdom "Greco-Bactrian kingdom").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin2013256-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Diadochen1.png/500px-Diadochen1.png)](/wiki/File:Diadochen1.png)
